pieces.title: Hot Cross Buns
pieces.description: An English street cry that became the classic first piano piece. Three notes, one hand position, and a tune you already know — the perfect place to connect fingers to sound.
pieces.teachingTips: Place your right thumb on middle C so fingers 1, 2 and 3 cover C, D and E. Say the words as you play to keep the rhythm honest, and let the half notes ring for their full two beats.

pieces.title: Mary Had a Little Lamb
pieces.description: One of the first melodies ever recorded on Edison's phonograph, and still one of the friendliest first tunes. It never leaves a five-finger hand position.
pieces.teachingTips: Watch for the repeated notes — let your finger rebound off the key like a small bounce rather than pressing harder each time. Count '1-2-3-4' out loud, and make the final whole note land gently.

pieces.title: Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star
pieces.description: A French folk tune so sturdy that Mozart wrote twelve variations on it. Its three matching phrases make musical form audible from the very first play-through.
pieces.teachingTips: The jump from C up to G is the only leap — prepare it by glancing at your hand before you play. Notice the middle section repeats itself exactly, so you already know half the piece.

pieces.title: Ode to Joy
pieces.description: The great hymn-like theme from the finale of Beethoven's Ninth Symphony, moved into C major so it sits under five fingers. Almost entirely stepwise, it teaches smooth, connected playing.
pieces.teachingTips: Play as smoothly as you can — imagine the notes are beads on a single thread. The dotted quarter in measures 4 and 8 is the only tricky rhythm: count '1-and-2-AND' and place the short note exactly on that last 'and'.

pieces.title: Jingle Bells
pieces.description: Pierpont wrote it for a Thanksgiving service, and it escaped into every winter since. The chorus is built almost entirely on repeated Es, which makes it a joyful repeated-note workout.
pieces.teachingTips: Keep the repeated Es light and springy — think of sleigh bells, not a hammer. In measure 5 your thumb stays on C while finger 4 reaches the F, so no position change is needed until the last line.

pieces.title: When the Saints Go Marching In
pieces.description: The New Orleans gospel standard that became a jazz anthem. Its opening phrase starts after a rest — a gentle first introduction to counting silence as carefully as sound.
pieces.teachingTips: Count the empty first beat out loud — 'ONE, C, E, F' — so the melody enters in the right place every time. Let the whole notes in measures 2 and 4 sing while you silently prepare the next phrase.

pieces.title: Amazing Grace
pieces.description: John Newton's 1779 hymn joined to the American tune 'New Britain'. In gentle 3/4 time with long singing lines, it is a first study in shaping a melody like a voice.
pieces.teachingTips: The tune begins on beat three — count 'one, two, D' to launch each phrase. Aim the crest of each phrase at its longest note and let the sound bloom there, then relax. Remember every F is F-sharp in this key.

pieces.title: Yankee Doodle
pieces.description: A Revolutionary-era marching tune with an unstoppable strut. The melody dips below middle C, so the right hand learns to travel while the left keeps a moving bass.
pieces.teachingTips: Keep a crisp, even quarter-note pulse — this is a march, so imagine feet hitting the ground. The reach down to G and A below middle C in measure 7 is the trickiest spot; practice that measure alone first.

pieces.title: Frère Jacques
pieces.description: The French round about a sleepy monk, sung in dozens of languages. Its four two-measure cells each repeat immediately, so every phrase gives you a free second chance.
pieces.teachingTips: The eighth notes in measures 5 and 6 move twice as fast as your left hand — say 'run-ning run-ning walk walk' to feel the relationship. Keep the left-hand halves like a calm bell tower underneath.

pieces.title: Happy Birthday to You
pieces.composer: Mildred J. Hill & Patty S. Hill
pieces.description: Possibly the most-sung melody on Earth, finally public domain. Every phrase launches from a two-note pickup, and the third phrase leaps a full octave — a small adventure for a level-two hand.
pieces.teachingTips: Each 'hap-py' is two quick eighth notes on beat three, launching into the next bar. Practice the octave leap in measure 6 by itself: thumb on low G, then swing your hand so finger 5 lands confidently on high G.

pieces.title: Minuet in G
pieces.description: The famous minuet from Anna Magdalena Bach's notebook — long credited to Bach, actually by Dresden organist Christian Petzold. Elegant, poised, and the classic gateway to Baroque style.
pieces.teachingTips: Think of a graceful dance in three: a gentle lean on beat one, lighter on two and three. Practice the eighth-note runs slowly with even fingers — no rushing downhill. The second half mirrors the first, differing only at the cadences.

pieces.title: Greensleeves
pieces.description: The Elizabethan ballad legend attributes to Henry VIII (it almost certainly isn't his). Its lilting 6/8 sway and touch of raised sharp make it a beautiful first minor-key piece.
pieces.teachingTips: Feel two gentle pulses per measure, not six — 'ONE-and-a TWO-and-a'. The D-sharp is the melody's moment of longing; lean into it slightly. Keep the left hand soft, like a lute in the background.

pieces.title: Für Elise (Theme)
pieces.description: Beethoven's bagatelle for a mysterious Elise, discovered forty years after his death. This arrangement presents the immortal opening theme with its rippling left-hand arpeggios.
pieces.teachingTips: The E to D-sharp shimmer should be feather-light — let the wrist stay loose. The left-hand broken chords answer the melody rather than compete with it; practice hands separately until each feels automatic, then combine at half speed.

pieces.title: Oh! Susanna
pieces.description: Foster's banjo-song that swept America during the Gold Rush. The dotted 'Su-san-na' rhythm is the whole personality of the piece — snappy, cheerful, and irresistible.
pieces.teachingTips: Master the dotted rhythm away from the piano first: clap 'LONG-short' until it swings naturally. The eighth-note pickups at the end of measures 1, 3 and 5 belong to the next phrase — breathe before them, not after.

pieces.title: Prelude in C (BWV 846, excerpt)
pieces.description: The opening of The Well-Tempered Clavier — no melody at all, just harmony set in perpetual motion. This simplified excerpt keeps Bach's chord progression in flowing eighth-note arpeggios.
pieces.teachingTips: Every note should match its neighbors in volume, like beads of water. Block each measure first — play all its notes together as a chord, feel the shape under your hand — then unroll it. Let the left hand ring underneath like a bell.

pieces.title: Canon in D (simplified)
pieces.description: The most borrowed bass line in history: eight notes that have carried three centuries of music. Over it, this arrangement layers Pachelbel's variations — long tones, then quarters, then flowing eighths.
pieces.teachingTips: Learn the left hand until you could play it asleep — it repeats identically every four measures. Then each right-hand variation is a new roof on the same house. Keep the eighths in measures 9-12 unhurried; the piece should feel like slow breathing.

pieces.title: Gymnopédie No. 1 (opening)
pieces.description: Satie's floating, melancholy waltz that seems to stand still. The left hand rocks between two chords forever; the melody drifts above like something remembered.
pieces.teachingTips: The whole piece is soft — practice playing the chords at a whisper without losing any notes. The left hand leaps from a low bass note to a mid-range chord; look at the chord before you jump, land like a cat. Never rush.

pieces.title: Moonlight Sonata, 1st mvt (opening)
pieces.description: Beethoven marked it 'like a fantasy'; a critic later saw moonlight on Lake Lucerne and the name stuck. The right hand murmurs unbroken triplets over deep octaves — music made of pure texture.
pieces.teachingTips: The triplets must be perfectly even and truly quiet — practice mezzo-forte first for accuracy, then fold the sound down to pianissimo. Let the low octaves ring their full length; they are the moonlight, the triplets only the water.

pieces.title: Prelude in E minor, Op. 28 No. 4 (excerpt)
pieces.description: Played at Chopin's own funeral: a melody that barely moves while the harmony sinks beneath it, one chromatic notch at a time. This excerpt preserves that slow harmonic descent.
pieces.teachingTips: The left-hand chords are a heartbeat: absolutely even, never accented. All the expression lives in the right hand's few notes — make each long tone swell slightly after it sounds. Follow the middle voice of the chords as it slides down by half steps.

pieces.title: Row, Row, Row Your Boat
pieces.description: The classic children's round that first appeared in mid-19th-century American songbooks. Its gentle 6/8 rocking mirrors the pull of oars, making it a perfect first taste of compound time.
pieces.teachingTips: Feel two big beats per measure rather than counting all six — sway 'ONE-two-three, TWO-two-three' like a boat on water. The busy 'merrily' measures are just the C-major chord bounced up and down; keep those eighth notes light and even.

pieces.title: London Bridge Is Falling Down
pieces.description: An English nursery rhyme whose singing-game roots reach back centuries. The tune circles a tight five-note range, so the whole piece stays comfortably under one hand.
pieces.teachingTips: Notice that the 'falling down' phrase repeats three times on almost the same notes — learn it once and you nearly know the piece. Keep a steady walking pulse and let each half note ring fully before the next phrase.

pieces.title: Lightly Row
pieces.description: A German folk melody long used as an early teaching piece, familiar from the Suzuki repertoire. Its two matching halves and clear phrases make musical shape easy to hear and feel.
pieces.teachingTips: Each phrase rises and then settles — lift slightly toward the top note and relax into the long note that ends it. Compare the first and second lines: they begin the same and only part ways at the very end.

pieces.title: Auld Lang Syne
pieces.description: Robert Burns set these words to an old Scots melody, and the world has sung it at every New Year since. Its gentle dotted rhythm and reaching phrases make it a warm study in expressive playing.
pieces.teachingTips: Each phrase begins on the final beat of the bar — count 'four, and' to launch cleanly into the downbeat. Play the dotted 'LONG-short' figures with an easy lilt, never rushed, and let the melody breathe at the top of each arch.

pieces.title: In the Hall of the Mountain King (theme)
pieces.description: From Grieg's music for Ibsen's 'Peer Gynt', depicting the hero fleeing a cave of trolls. The creeping theme climbs the same few notes over and over, growing faster and louder — here presented at a steady stalking pace.
pieces.teachingTips: Play the eighth notes short and sneaky, like tiptoeing footsteps. The whole tune is one small idea repeated a step higher each time, so lock the fingering of the first bar and simply shift it up. Keep the left-hand bass heavy and grounded beneath the creeping right hand.

pieces.title: Morning Mood (opening)
pieces.description: The sunrise that opens Grieg's 'Peer Gynt' Suite, passed between flute and oboe over shimmering strings. Its gently rocking pentatonic melody is one of the most recognizable dawns in all of music.
pieces.teachingTips: Shape the melody like a slow breath in and out — rise softly toward each high note and ease back down. The tune is entirely built from a five-note pattern that keeps folding back on itself, so listen for how each phrase gently answers the last. Remember every F is F-sharp.

pieces.title: Habanera from Carmen (theme)
pieces.description: Carmen's seductive entrance aria, built on a slinking chromatic line that slides downward one half-step at a time. Bizet borrowed the swaying habanera dance rhythm, giving the melody its irresistible pull.
pieces.teachingTips: Practice the chromatic descent slowly, naming each half step so the fingers learn the tight downward slide. Keep the left-hand habanera pattern steady and slightly detached — it is the dance floor under the singer's line. Let the melody lean and linger for a touch of Carmen's swagger.
